You are an expert EU Horizon Europe proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions — a photonics and NIR spectroscopy SME in Barcelona.

Write in this exact style:
- First person plural throughout: we, our, the project will, we will develop
- Vary sentence length: short declarative statement, then longer supporting sentence
- Domain-specific vocabulary with grounded numbers and reasoning chains
- For work plan sections: bold task labels — **Task 2.1** — with partner attribution: (Lead: IRIS; Partners: X, Y)
- For objectives: numbered, specific, measurable, linked to TRL advancement
- NEVER use: poised to deliver, transformative outcomes, holistic approach, dynamic interplay, at the forefront, leverages its expertise
Write Section 1.1 Objectives and Ambition for a Horizon Europe proposal on: CO2 capture, methanol-to-olefins conversion, and industrial carbon utilisation

1.1 Objectives and ambition 

 
 
        HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 
Efficient capture / purification / utilisation of CO2 for the production of competitive products 
Page 3 of 48 
A vision for a decentralised Industrial Symbiosis: The overarching objective of CO2HESION is to demonstrate 
an intensified, two-step CCU platform for the high-efficiency production of an ethylene/propylene mixture and 
circular Li2CO3 from industrial flue gas. This is achieved through the in-situ coupling of upgraded V-CaL capture 
with a patented CSF thermocatalytic reactor and AEM electrolysis and, in parallel, with the utilisation of the 
CO2 in ACC’s selective chemical-free lithium extraction process.  
The approach combines a set of advanced yet modular technologies, including V-CaL capture, CSF conversion, and 
black mass valorisation, which are effective as standalone solutions while also being designed for flexible integration 
into existing industrial processes. By bypassing conventional CO2 logistics, the project aims to validate a TRL6 
industrial symbiosis model that increases energy efficiency by >40% and reduces operation expenses by 30% 
compared to fossil-based benchmarks. The project will deliver a de-risked, economically viable blueprint for the 
rapid rollout of CCU plants across European industrial parks, turning CO2 from a liability into a competitive resource. 
This vision is realised through three strategic pillars: 
1.Process Intensification (PI) through Convergence: Replacing 5+ stages of traditional CCU with a streamlined, 
two-step "Capture-to-Product" architecture, enabled by a) the coupling of the one-step V-CaL capture and 
purification technology with patented CSF reactor, and b) the captured-CO2-assisted conversion of battery black mass 
into lithium carbonate. 
2.Renewable Energy Synchronisation: AEM electrolysis enables the conversion of intermittent renewable 
electricity into a stable chemical feedstock (green hydrogen), proving that heavy industry can operate with a 
fluctuating green grid. In parallel, all core CO2HESION technologies are based on electrified operation, making the 
overall platform compatible with renewable electricity integration. 
3.Multi-Sectorial Circularity: Demonstrating that a single capture hub can simultaneously feed the Petrochemical 
(Olefins) and Mineral (Lithium Carbonate) value chains, creating a "symbiotic cluster" that reduces Scope 3 
emissions and strengthens European strategic autonomy. 
In order to deliver on CO2HESION’s vision and ambition, the following quantified and measurable objectives 
will be fulfilled over the course of a 48-month workplan.   
Table 1- CO2HESION Objectives  
O1 
To develop and demonstrate a modular and scalable “Capture-to-Product” CCU platform, enabling 
both stand-alone and fully integrated on-site operation, where a single CO2 capture unit feeds 
multiple chemical and mineral value chains through independent, site-adaptable process blocks, fully 
compatible with and responsive to renewable energy inputs across all electrified subsystems (capture, 
electrolysis and conversion). 
Outcomes: Reduced industrial carbon footprint; increased energy security; Circular economy integration; waste-
to-resource transition; Integration of intermittent renewable energy sources into industrial production systems 
KPI 1.1: >40% reduction in specific energy consumption (GJ/t) vs.

Write Section 1.3 Methodology for a Horizon Europe proposal on: CO2 capture, methanol-to-olefins conversion, and industrial carbon utilisation

methodology for process intensification (shifting the equilibrium by 
in-situ removal of reaction by-products like water) state-of-the-art implementations are largely restricted to fixed-
bed configurations. These conventional systems operate in transient, batch-like cycles, requiring periodic reactor 
shutdown or complex multi-bed switching for sorbent regeneration once saturation is reached. This lack of 
operational continuity remains a significant barrier to the cost-effective, large-scale industrialisation of CO2 
valorisation. 
 
1 V-CaL patent: “Vacuum Swing Calcination Process To Produce High Purity CO2 from CaCO3”, EP 4098 613 A1, Juan Carlos Abanades, Borja Arias 
Rozada, Jose Ramon Fernandez García. 2022. * The technology was originally invented in the CSIC-INCAR and was transferred to TRSA in 2024 
2 “Development of a new CO2 capture process with vacuum-assisted calcination for the direct production of pure CO2”, José Ramón Fernández*, Arturo 
Barragán1, Miriam Díaz, Ramón Pericet-Cámara, Juan Carlos Abanades. Journal of Cleaner Production. In press.  

 
 
        HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 
Efficient capture / purification / utilisation of CO2 for the production of competitive products 
Page 7 of 48 
Beyond SOTA: CO2HESION overcomes these limitations by deploying a proprietary3 Continuous Sorbent 
Feeding (CSF) fluidized-bed reactor (figure 3), a breakthrough configuration originally pioneered by UNIZAR. 
Unlike static systems, the CSF architecture exploits the physical and hydrodynamic segregation of dual-solid phases 
(the tandem catalyst and the specialized sorbent) within a single fluidized environment. By precisely tuning the 
particle size and density of the two solids, the system enables the preferential and continuous withdrawal of the 
saturated sorbent for external regeneration without interrupting the catalytic conversion. 
This configuration has already achieved successful experimental validation for CO2 hydrogenation to Methanol4; 
CO2HESION will extend this state of the art to the more complex synthesis of light olefins with the implementation 
of advanced tandem catalytic materials. UNIZAR will identify silico-alumino-phosphate (SAPO)-based olefin-
selective zeolites within a single particle architecture. By achieving a steady-state sorption-enhanced environment, 
the CSF reactor minimizes the Thermal Footprint of the process and significantly increases the single-pass conversion 
efficiency. This represents a paradigm shift in reactor engineering, moving from discrete batch processes to a 
continuous, intensified platform capable of meeting the rigorous throughput and stability requirements of the 
European petrochemical and ceramic industries. 
HydroLiC: The global lithium value chain in 2026 is characterized by a 
high degree of geographical concentration, with over 90% of production 
originating from mineral mining in Australia and China or brine 
extraction in the "Lithium Triangle" of Chile and Argentina. This 
external dependency creates significant supply chain vulnerabilities for 
the European Union, particularly as the demand for lithium-ion batteries 
(LIBs) is projected to reach 116.1 kt for NMC and LMNO chemistries 
to meet the 2030 climate targets. While the battery sector is the primary 
driver of the volatile lithium market, which saw prices fluctuate from 
over $50,000/tn in 2022 to approximately $15,500/tn in 2024, lithium 
remains equally indispensable for the European ceramic and glass 
industries. In these sectors, lithium serves as a critical fluxing agent in 
the production of high-performance frits and glazes. With Europe 
producing approximately 1.25 million tons of frits annually, primarily 
concentrated in industrial clusters in Spain, Italy, and Turkey, the secure 
supply of Li2CO3 is a prerequisite for maintaining the competitiveness 
of the EU’s ceramic sector. 
Current state-of-the-art Li2CO3 production within the EU focuses on 
secondary streams such as end-of-l

Write Section 2.3 Communication Plan for a Horizon Europe proposal on: CO2 capture, methanol-to-olefins conversion, and industrial carbon utilisation

Communication Plan and Strategy: The CO2HESION communication strategy is aimed to position CO2HESION 
as a relevant EU initiative contributing to industrial decarbonisation and climate neutrality. We aim to make 
our project a reference for integrated CO2 capture and utilisation, with a focus on its integrated “capture-to-product” 
platform, by ensuring visibility, explaining the value of the approach, and connecting with stakeholders that influence 
uptake. This effort will be underpinned by solid content, pushed out through the most suitable and relevant channels, 
and timed to ensure coherent and impactful outreach throughout the project lifecycle.  
The main objectives are to: 1) establish a clear and recognisable identity for CO2HESION and ensure visibility at 
European level; 2) communicate the project’s core concept (an integrated “capture-to-product” platform) in a way 
that is accessible beyond specialist audiences; 3) highlight the relevance of CO2HESION for industrial 
decarbonisation, circular raw materials, and energy integration; 4) engage with EU initiatives, industrial ecosystems, 
and related projects to strengthen positioning and synergies; 5) contribute to the visibility of EU-funded innovation 
in CO2 utilisation and industrial transformation. 
These objectives are delivered through a set of communication tools and channels (Table 13):  
Table 13- Communication KPIs (CO2HESION)

        HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 
Efficient capture / purification / utilisation of CO2 for the production of competitive products 
Page 25 of 48 
industrial transition narrative 
mentions 
A consistent visual identity will be developed at the beginning of the project to ensure recognisability across all 
outputs (incl. upgraded professional logo, templates, and communication materials aligned with Horizon Europe 
requirements), and for consistency and clarity in how the CO2HESION concept is presented across channels and 
contexts. The website will be professional, easy to access and navigate and it will be regularly maintained to reflect 
project status and progress. We also aim to generate video content, which is a highly consumed format across all 
target groups and is now a democratised and cost-effective endeavour with a whole host of new AI tools at our 
disposal. The key will be to ensure high quality, effective content and consistent messaging, targeted channels, and 
a focus on real industrial relevance. CO2HESION will use EC platforms (e.g. CORDIS, Horizon Magazine, among 
others) and participate in relevant EU initiatives to increase visibility and ensure alignment with broader EU 
decarbonisation and industrial strategies. 
Dissemination Plan and Strategy: Dissemination in CO2HESION focuses on the effective transfer of knowledge, 
results, and innovations to all relevant stakeholders who can benefit from and further develop the project outcomes. 
The dissemination strategy is designed to ensure that project results are accessible, understandable, and actionable, 
ultimately supporting their uptake, replication, and potential

Write Section 3.1 Work Plan and Work Packages for a Horizon Europe proposal on: CO2 capture, methanol-to-olefins conversion, and industrial carbon utilisation. Use bold Task X.Y labels with (Lead: X; Partners: Y, Z) attribution.

3.1 Work plan and resources  
Overall Strategy of the work plan: The CO2HESION work plan has been designed based on the project objectives 
and consists of 13 interlinked Work Packages (WPs) that support the development, integration, and validation of the 
CO2HESION platform, as illustrated in the PERT diagram (Figure 7), which defines the logical sequencing, 
dependencies, and feedback loops between the WPs.  
The work plan will start with the definition of the industrial context and system requirements in WP1 (Industrial 
process and cluster specification), which will establish the boundary conditions, use cases, and reference scenarios 
for the project. This provides the basis for the development of core materials and components in WP2 (Material 
Development for CO2 capture resilience and utilisation), followed by their validation at laboratory scale in WP3 
(Lab-scale validation of CO2 Capture and Utilisation systems). 
Building on these results, the project moves to system-level implementation through WP4 (Pilot Plant Design and 
Construction), where the integrated platform is engineered and assembled. This is followed by WP5 (Process 
Demonstration under Dynamic/Renewable Loading), which validates the system under industrially relevant and 
dynamic operating conditions, including variable energy inputs and real process constraints. 
In parallel, WP6 (Digital Twin, System Modelling and Industrial Upscaling) provides the system-level modelling 
and digital infrastructure required to support monitoring, analysis, and optimisation of the integrated platform, as 
well as the assessment of scale-up scenarios. Complementary to this, WP7 (Sustainability, Techno-Economic, and 
Environmental Assessment) evaluates the performance of the system in terms of cost, environmental impact, and 
industrial feasibility. 
Project impact will be consolidated through a comprehensive series of activities in WP8–WP10 that will cover 
communication, dissemination, exploitation, and standardisation across the three reporting periods, ensuring 
continuity and progressive development of impact-related actions. Coordination and governance are ensured through 
WP11-13 ensuring effective project management across the full duration of the project. 
 

 
 
        HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 
Efficient capture / purification / utilisation of CO2 for the production of competitive products 
Page 31 of 48 
 
Figure 7- CO2HESION workflow to TRL6 showing the progression from experimental validation (WP1–WP3), through 
modelling and interface definition, to integrated pilot demonstration (WP4–WP5) and Digital Twin-supported optimisation and 
scale-up (WP6), with iterative feedback loops enabling a data-driven engineering cycle. 
Overall, the work plan will facilitate a progression from specification and component development to integration, 
demonstration, and assessment, ensuring a coherent pathway from TRL4-6 and future scale-up. Moreover, work plan 
is directly aligned with the key requirements of the MAT-PROD-31 call topic, as summarised below. 
Table 16- CO2HESION work plan aligned with call topic requirements. 
Call requirement 
Work Package alignment 
Efficient CO2 capture and purification

Competitive CO2-based products 
WP5 (olefins and Li2CO3 validation) 
Renewable energy integration 
WP5 (demonstration) and WP6 (system modelling) 
Digitalisation and process modelling 
WP6 (digital twin and scale-up) 
Industrial symbiosis and cluster integration 
WP1 (specification) and WP8–WP10 (exploitation and replication)

(D) and milestones (MS).

Write Section 3.3 Consortium as a Whole for a Horizon Europe proposal on: CO2 capture, methanol-to-olefins conversion, and industrial carbon utilisation

consortium as a whole   
The CO2HESION consortium consists of a complementary team of research, technology, industrial and innovation 
ecosystem partners covering the full value chain required to develop and validate an integrated CO2 capture, 
purification and utilisation (CCU) system for the production of competitive chemical products. The partners combine 
expertise in carbon capture, catalytic conversion, reactor and process engineering, industrial integration, 
digitalisation, system optimisation, life cycle assessment and techno-economic analysis. 
Complementarity and Value Chain Coverage: The consortium covers the full CCU value chain, from CO2 
generation and capture to conversion, validation, and end-use. Industrial CO2 streams are provided by TOR, enabling 
real operating conditions from the outset. Capture, purification, and system integration are addressed by TRSA, 
supported by material development and catalytic conversion from UNIZAR and ACC. Process validation and 
industrial compatibility are ensured through ORL and TOR, covering both petrochemical and ceramic applications. 
System optimisation, monitoring, and scale-up are supported by IRIS through Digital Twin and data-driven 
approaches, while INEGI provides techno-economic, environmental, and sustainability assessment, ADM brings 
expertise in the implementation of the EU Innovation Fund’s GHG Avoidance Methodology. Exploitation, and 
market alignment are driven by ICP. ADM, and BOP, ensuring that technical results translate into deployable 
solutions, along with standardisation activities (UNE).  
Industrial and Exploitation

chains, enabling CO2 utilisation in secondary raw material recovery. INEGI supports industrial decision-making 
through techno-economic and environmental validation, while ICP and BOP ensure that exploitation pathways, IP

management, and market positioning (ADM) and standardisation (UNE) are addressed from early stages. This 
combined industrial, technological, and commercial capacity ensures a clear pathway to scale-up, industrial uptake, 
and replication across European industrial clusters. 
Roles and Contributions: As summarised in Table 17 each partner has a clearly defined role aligned with their 
expertise and resources, as summarised below, and whereby all partners occupy roles consistent with their capabilities 
and have access to the infrastructure required to deliver their contributions. 
Table 17. Partner Expertise, roles and critical infrastructure 
Técnicas Reunidas (TRSA) is one of the world's leading engineering and construction companies. Over 60 years’ experience 
designing and building plants in the five continents. With strong history in Oil&Gas and Energy projects, current activity is 
shifting towards energy transition projects, such as low-carbon hydrogen (blue and green) or ammonia plants, carbon capture, 
biofuel-based power generation and circular economy. TRSA is also a recognised technology developer and licenser in metal 
extract

business case and exploitation pathways for CO2HESION solution; Defined IP

Relation to the work program: CO2HESION will develop a fully integrated CCU platform enabled by V-CaL, 
CSF and HydroLiC intensification. It is fully aligned with the scope of HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 
call. By integrating high-efficiency CO2 capture and purification technology with: (i) a patented thermocatalytic 
conversion reactor and (ii) a battery recycling route for the production of recycled Li2CO3, the project directly 
supports the Processes4Planet Roadmap and the EU’s transition toward a circular, net-zero industrial base. Our 
approach focuses on Process Intensification as the primary driver for competitiveness, moving beyond the current 

 
 
        HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 
Efficient capture / purification / utilisation of CO2 for the production of competitive products 
Page 5 of 48 
fragmented SOA to deliver a streamlined, on-site "Capture-to-Product" architecture. The following table maps how 
the project’s technical objectives (including the 40% increase in energy efficiency and the 30% reduction in operation 
expenses) directly respond to the key requirements of the Work Programme and the strategic needs of the European 
petrochemical and mineral sectors.

Energy Efficiency Increase: By thermally linking the various sub-systems, 
such as utilising the obtained heat from V-CaL pure CO2 for CSF thermal 
conditions, as well as exothermic heat of chemical synthesis to assist in sorbent 
regeneration, the project radically reduces the overall energy penalty associated 
with CCU. By integrating V-CaL capture with the patented CSF reactor, and 
battery recycling line to produce Li2CO3, we eliminate intermediate 
liquefaction and transport, drastically cutting the energy penalty up to 40%.

olefins for ORL (Petrochemicals) and Li2CO3 from EoL battery black mass for 
re-use in ceramics and battery applications (active cathode material)
